one_line_takeaway: Hawk swaps garbled circuits for secret-shared lookup tables to compute exact ML activations in two-server secure computation, hitting plaintext accuracy (96.6% MNIST NN) with up to 688x speedup over SecureML, and its reusable-table variant HawkMulti buys huge storage savings by accepting dX-private access-pattern leakage.
key_finding: Hawk matches plaintext accuracy while being dramatically faster than the garbled-circuit baseline SecureML. On MNIST the neural network reaches 96.6% accuracy in 15 epochs, versus the ~93.4% cap reported by prior secure-training works (SecureML/SecureNN/ABY3) that use a non-standard Softmax approximation; the gain is attributed to accurate standard activations. NN training is up to 688x faster than SecureML (WAN, vs client-aided) and PPLR up to ~9x faster. HawkMulti's dX-privacy relaxation cuts lookup-table storage from HawkSingle's 407.5 GB to 4.1 GB per party (NN) with negligible accuracy loss at moderate privacy budgets.
core_contribution: Hawk introduces two secret-shared lookup-table protocols, HawkSingle and HawkMulti, for computing non-linear activation functions (Sigmoid, ReLU/DReLU, Softmax) inside privacy-preserving ML training/inference in a two-server (2PC), client-aided secure-computation setting. The core idea is to replace Yao's garbled circuits (the standard but expensive tool for non-linear functions in prior work) with precomputed secret-shared lookup tables indexed by a securely-derived deterministic key, giving both speed and plaintext-level accuracy. HawkSingle consumes one table per function evaluation and is leakage-free (perfect security in the semi-honest model). HawkMulti is the paper's headline contribution: it enables table REUSE using elliptic-curve (ECDLP-based) deterministic keying, trading a small, quantified access-pattern leakage that the authors prove is bounded by epsilon-dX-privacy (metric differential privacy) for large reductions in offline/storage cost. Secondary contributions: an alternate fixed-point DReLU representation that shrinks its lookup table, a two-table (exp + inverse) method for accurate secure Softmax (claimed first accurate secure Softmax), and formal simulation-based security proofs.
threat_model: Clients secret-share their (horizontally or vertically partitioned) data between two untrusted but non-colluding servers P0 and P1 that perform training/inference. Adversary is semi-honest (honest-but-curious): follows the protocol but tries to learn honest clients' data. It may corrupt at most one of the two servers and any subset of clients, PROVIDED corrupted clients do not collude with the servers (a stated limitation of the client-aided offline phase). Security goal: the adversary learns only the data of corrupted clients and the final trained model, nothing about honest clients' inputs. HawkSingle leaks nothing beyond this. HawkMulti additionally reveals a perturbed histogram of lookup-table access patterns (a pseudorandom key kappa per access, never tied to a specific input x), and this leakage function is proven to be local epsilon-dX-private via a geometric-mechanism noise term. The relaxation deliberately avoids ORAM's inherent Omega(log n) overhead and PIR/FSS's Omega(n) overhead.
limitations: (1) Semi-honest only; no security against malicious/active adversaries. (2) Client-aided offline phase requires that clients never collude with the servers. (3) HawkSingle's storage is prohibitive for NN training (407.5 GB per party for the MNIST DNN; 36.62 GB for logistic regression), so it is practical only for logistic regression and inference. (4) HawkMulti removes that storage barrier only by accepting access-pattern leakage; it is NOT fully oblivious, and the privacy is a relaxed metric-DP guarantee rather than cryptographic indistinguishability of access patterns, with an accuracy/privacy/storage trade-off governed by epsilon_T (e.g. accuracy dips at epsilon_T = 0.0005 for LR and epsilon_T = 0.02 for the NN). (5) Evaluation covers fully-connected DNNs only; CNNs are deferred to future work. (6) Direct comparisons are mostly against SecureML because it is the only prior two-server client-aided training work; comparisons to 3PC systems (SecureNN, ABY3, Falcon) and to Pika/SIRNN (sigmoid, appendix) are across differing settings. (7) Quantization to 16-bit fixed point is required for tractable table sizes.

datasets_used: MNIST (digit classification; also used as 0-vs-non-0 binary task for LR)
quantitative_results: PPLR MNIST accuracy: HawkSingle/HawkMulti(eps_T=0.01) 99.21%, TensorFlow 99.19%, SecureML 98.62%
PPNN MNIST accuracy: 96.6% in 15 epochs (matches TensorFlow 96.6%), vs prior works' ~93.4% cap; surpasses 90% in under 1.06 epochs
NN training WAN: up to 688x faster than SecureML client-aided (391-688x), 159-281x vs SecureML server-aided; LAN online 26-144x faster
PPLR: up to 9x faster than SecureML (abstract); text reports 3.4x LAN and 8.7x WAN on MNIST

Table 5 (PPNN MNIST, LAN, |B|=128, rMulti=100): HawkSingle offline 0.47h / online 0.02h / 407.5 GB storage; HawkMulti(eps_T=1) offline 0.35h / online 0.11h / 4.1 GB; SecureML server-aided offline 80.5h / online 1.17h; Quotient online 8.72h
Table 5 WAN online hours: HawkSingle 0.21, HawkMulti(eps_T=1) 0.37, SecureML server-aided 59, Quotient 50.74, Falcon 3.76, SecureNN 7.83
Table 3 (PPLR MNIST): HawkSingle LAN 3.2s / WAN 70.2s / offline 57.58s / 36.62 GB; HawkMulti(eps_T=0.01) LAN 4s / WAN 144s / offline 18.3s / 0.37 GB; SecureML LAN 10.84s / WAN 612.78s
vs Quotient (2PC): PPNN online 79-436x faster LAN, 137-242x faster WAN; vs 3PC (SecureNN/ABY3/Falcon): up to 52x LAN, 37x WAN
Secure inference (Table 6, 100 MNIST samples): HawkSingle online up to 2.5x faster than SecureML for both LR and NN
Alternate DReLU representation reduces the DReLU lookup table by a factor of 2^13 (Section 5.1); lookup tables bounded to 2^16 entries via 16-bit fixed point
Elliptic-curve optimizations cut offline scalar multiplications from m*2^18 to m+1, and reduce online steps to one scalar multiplication per party
ECC needs ~12x smaller keys than RSA for 128-bit security
baselines_compared: SecureML [58] (primary; 2PC client-aided and server-aided, garbled circuits)
Pika [73] and SIRNN [63] (sigmoid computation, Appendix F)
